meta.title: Perplexity AI
meta.subtitle: The Answer Engine
meta.tagline: It solved Google's link overload. Then created a new problem.

intro.summary: Perplexity sits at a unique position in the AI landscape - not fully a chatbot, not a full-fledged search engine. It is more of an answer engine, which can synthesize and process real-time web content into cited conversational responses. In a way, it solves for the problems of Google Search's link overload and ChatGPT's hallucination in a go. Google Search generates links of all pages which can even remotely hold the answers to your questions. Perplexity takes that information and generates conversational responses out of it, not overwhelming users with bunch of pages. Since it cites all its responses, it tries to solve for the hallucination problem also.

personas.title: Students & Academics
personas.description: Citation based answers, academic filters, access to peer-reviewed sources.

personas.title: Researchers & Analysts
personas.description: Real-time synthesis of current data with verifiable sources. Deep Research and Spaces features create persistent knowledge environments.

personas.title: Journalists & Fact Checkers
personas.description: Real-time source verification helps with rapid background research of developing stories.

personas.title: Content Writers
personas.description: Strong for research & ideation, falls short for long form generation and creative output.

personas.title: Developers & Engineers
personas.description: Useful for quick technical lookups, but not a coding companion.

what_works.title: Citation First Transparency
points: Helps build trust, critical for any kind of research, academic, or fact checking.

what_works.title: Source Filtering
points: Users have the ability to filter which source they want the results from - social, academic, or web.

what_works.title: Deep Research & Spaces
points: Provides pro users with organized research environments.
Users can create an AI "expert" in a specific domain by uploading documents and setting context.

what_works.title: Multi-Model Flexibility
points: Perplexity enables users to choose between different models for different tasks - all within a single interface.

what_doesnt_work.title: Billing and Plan Failures with Zero Human Support
what_doesnt_work.description: I am surprised to find out something this basic is a #1 problem for an otherwise complex product. Users have complained about limits downgrading mid-subscription, double-charging, or subscriptions being cancelled without any refunds or reasonings. Raising complaints results in auto-generated responses with no clear justification or path forward.

what_doesnt_work.title: Hallucination with Attribution Problem
what_doesnt_work.description: What was designed as a way to manage hallucination problem ended up creating a deeper problem. Perplexity does cite sources of the responses, but the citations have been known to be identified as incorrect. This is an even bigger problem because it makes the users believe that the responses are sourced and cited, but it is hallucinating with authority.

what_doesnt_work.title: Reproduction of Licensed Content
what_doesnt_work.description: By early 2026, Perplexity is facing lawsuits from various news corps on the ground that it is performing unauthorized scraping and reproduction of copyrighted content without licensing it. Its entire business model is under business review at the moment. This is not just a legal trouble for Perplexity, but for the users also who rely on such content for academic and legal research.

what_doesnt_work.title: Stateless Sessions
what_doesnt_work.description: It treats every query as stateless and often forgets the context of previous queries. Research workflows require building on prior context across sessions, this is a genuine limitation.

opportunities.title: FIX CUSTOMER SERVICE!
opportunities.description: Rebuild the process ground up. Align the billing plans with the policy and announce transparently; whenever policy is updated mid-subscription, send an informational emails to the customers beforehand and give them an option to opt-out with refund of the balance payment; build a team of human agents to handle disputes, issues, and concerns. For a company whose foundational promise is trust (cited responses), this is too basic a miss! No fancy tech can replace honesty and dignity in service!

opportunities.title: Confidence Scoring in Attribution
opportunities.description: While hallucination is an industry wide problem, we can still build for trust. At the end of every response, add whether the answer is directly sourced or is interpreted from multiple sources. If it is interpreted, users will know to double-check the source to verify the correctness. Users should not be in the blind; let them know when they are reading a conclusion, not a fact.

opportunities.title: I Don't Know as a Feature
opportunities.description: Train the model to say "sources don't clearly answer this" rather than synthesizing a response when confidence is low. This is actually much more feasible for Perplexity to do than for pure LLMs, given its search-first architecture.

opportunities.title: Persistent Memory for Research Workflows
opportunities.description: A research memory feature where Perplexity can retain context of the previous conversations within a space would dramatically increase the stickiness of the users who run long sessions. This will evolve Perplexity from an advanced search tool to a true research partner!

items.name: Billing Tickets per 1K Subscribers
items.description: Number of billing-related support tickets per 1,000 subscribers per month.

items.name: Time to Resolution
items.description: Average time to resolution for billing disputes. Target: under 48 hours.

items.name: Human vs. Auto Resolution Rate
items.description: % of disputes resolved by human agent vs. auto-response.

items.name: Escalation Rate
items.description: % of bot-handled tickets escalated to human agents. Distinguishes genuine bot resolution from deflection.

items.name: Refund Approval Rate
items.description: Refund approval rate and average processing time.

items.name: Billing Churn Rate
items.description: Subscription churn rate, specifically tracking churn reason codes - billing vs. product vs. competition.

items.name: Source Click-Through Rate
items.description: Click-through rate on cited sources, segmented by "directly sourced" vs. "interpreted" labels. Tells you if users are trusting interpreted answers blindly.

items.name: Interpreted vs. Sourced Response Rate
items.description: % of responses flagged as "interpreted synthesis" vs. "directly sourced". Tracks model honesty over time.

items.name: User Correction Rate
items.description: How often users flag a cited response as incorrect.

items.name: I Don't Know Response Rate
items.description: Paired with a satisfaction score on those responses - because users need to feel helped, not abandoned.

items.name: NPS by Segment
items.description: Net Promoter Score segmented by free vs. Pro vs. Enterprise. The enterprise NPS is probably catastrophically low right now.

items.name: Session Depth on Research Tasks
items.description: Are users completing multi-step research flows or dropping off? Measures if the product is actually delivering on its core JTBD.

pm_take: Perplexity built something genuinely useful - an answer engine that collapses Google's link overload into cited, conversational responses. But it has created a more dangerous problem than the one it solved. ChatGPT hallucinates with no sources. Perplexity hallucinates with authority - fake answers wearing the logos of real publishers. That is a trust collapse waiting to happen at scale. The billing failures compound this: for a product whose entire value proposition is trust, charging users incorrectly and refusing to fix it with a human is a product contradiction. No fancy tech can replace honesty and dignity in service. The path forward is clear - confidence scoring, an honest 'I don't know', persistent research memory, and a customer service team that treats users like the researchers they are.
